title: Signal Dry Bulk Weekly Report
summary:
Main
The weaker momentum of freight rates persists for all the weeks of January with Chinese iron ore prices posting again a downward trend. Last Friday, there were signs for an upward weekly gain in iron ore prices amid hopes for strong Chinese demand followed by the government's fresh stimulus measures, however, the opening of the week brings a dip in prices. Spot prices of iron ore with 63.5% iron content for delivery to Tianjin traded at $130/t at the end of January after rising as high as $133 last week, the strongest level since September 7th.

children.title: Main
children.summary: The weaker momentum of freight rates persists for all the weeks of January with Chinese iron ore prices posting again a downward trend. Last Friday, there were signs for an upward weekly gain in iron ore prices amid hopes for strong Chinese demand followed by the government's fresh stimulus measures, however, the opening of the week brings a dip in prices.

children.title: SECTION 1 - FREIGHT - Market Rates ($/t) - Weaker
children.summary:
‘The Big Picture' - Capesize and Panamax Bulkers and Smaller Ship Sizes
Image reference: ch1.png | assets/2022-01-26_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch1_3eb86dd9ba07.png
Capesize Brazil to NChina rates dropped below $18/t (vs ~$30/t Week 49, 2021) and Panamax Continent to Far East rates are now ~$43/t vs $(53/t at Week 49 of last year. In the supramax segment, Indo ECI rates keep decreasing trend at the levels of less than $20t, and hover at levels less than $16.5/t for the current week.

children.title: Number of Vessels - Increasing
children.summary:
Supply Trend Lines for Key Load Areas
Image reference: ch2.png | assets/2022-01-26_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch2_3528939b585f.png
The number of vessels sailing in ballast status is still evolving above the average trend line for the handysize and Panamax segment, at levels of above 65 for handysize and 100 for Panamax. In the Capesize segment, there are some signs of a slight drop this week, with the number moving to less than 70 ballasters, compared to around 80, in the previous three weeks of January.

children.title: Decreasing
children.summary:
Image reference: ch3.png | assets/2022-01-26_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch3_506678605583.png
Looking at the evolution of demand growth, the current fundamentals for the iron market and Chinese economy remain in a silent mode a few days before the Chinese New Year. Thus, the Capesize picture has not yet shown any sign for a firm increase and in the Panamax segment, the situation remains critical with the Indonesian gradual relaxation of the coal ban fuelling a sense of optimism.

children.title: Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
section_path: Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
children.section_path_text: Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
children.summary:
Image reference: ch4.png | assets/2022-01-26_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch4_40e5c4ca15f3.png
The number of congestion remains hovering below the average of ~1120 vessels since Week 46, 2021. The last days of January seem to evolve with a soft increasing pace, which is mainly stemming from the handysize and Capesize segment.
